results.possibleIdentity: Michael William Gilbert (born 1954) is an American electronic music composer and recording artist who bridges the gap between art, technology, and science. He studied electrical engineering at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and electronic music at the Boston School of Electronic Music, where he later served as an instructor. His artistic career began in the 1970s, characterized by the use of synthesizers, custom sound synthesis systems (some of which he designed), and traditional instruments such as flutes and percussion. He is known for a collaborative approach to music, often working with other experimental musicians and utilizing cutting-edge technology to create 'humanized' electronic music. Gilbert has served as the technical director for electronic music studios at various institutions, including Amherst College, Smith College, Hampshire College, and the University of Massachusetts Amherst. His first solo LP, 'Moving Pictures,' was released in 1978, followed by 'The Call' in 1980.

Michael 'Mike' Gilbert (1947–2000) was a prominent American science fiction and fantasy illustrator and author active from the late 1960s. He graduated from the Rochester Institute of Technology (RIT) in 1970, a university known for its focus on the intersection of art and technology. Gilbert gained early recognition as a prolific fan artist in fanzines like 'Locus,' winning the Hugo Award for Best Fan Artist in 1971. He transitioned to professional work in the 1970s, creating cover art and interior illustrations for magazines such as 'Analog Science Fiction/Science Fact' and 'Fantasy & Science Fiction,' as well as illustrations for early role-playing games like 'Citadel' and 'Bunnies & Burrows.' He served as the art director for 'If' magazine and co-authored/illustrated 'The Day of the Ness' (1975) with Andre Norton.

results.possibleIdentity: Steve Johnson (also known as Stephen C. Johnson) was a computer scientist and engineer at Bell Laboratories who was active in the development of early computer music in the late 1960s. In 1968, he wrote a sound editing and processing program called 'SuperSplicer' for the composer Vladimir Ussachevsky during Ussachevsky's residency at Bell Labs. This collaboration was part of the activities fostered by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which aimed to pair artists with engineers to explore new creative technologies. Johnson is also well known in the history of computing for developing the UNIX utilities Yacc and Lint, and for co-authoring the first port of the UNIX operating system.

Steve Johnson was a visual and mixed-media artist active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, specifically associated with the Portland, Oregon chapter of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). His work during this period involved creating photographic images on unconventional surfaces such as spoons, dried leaves, and weathered boards. He was a featured artist in the 'OBJECTS' exhibition organized in 1970 by Jack Eyerly, who was the founder and coordinator of the Portland E.A.T. group. Johnson's artistic contributions are documented in the Jack Eyerly collection on Pacific Northwest Art, which preserves the history of experimental and technology-based art in that region.

Steven Maxwell Johnson (born 1938) is an American cartoonist, futurist, and artist who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, initially working as a city planner. In 1973, he began a long career as an editorial cartoonist for the Sierra Club and later The Sacramento Bee, specializing in whimsical and predictive drawings of futuristic inventions and technological solutions. His work, which explores the intersection of design, technology, and social needs, aligns with the interdisciplinary goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), though he is primarily recognized for his books such as 'What the World Needs Now' and his conceptual 'patent-pending' inventions.

results.possibleIdentity: Jill Rosenberg is an artist active since at least the early 1960s, known for creating works that emphasize themes of global unity. She is recognized for the original artwork titled "The Earth is But One Country," which was used for a first edition collector's plate produced for UNICEF (released around 1979-1980). This piece features a colorful design of children from around the world in traditional attire encircling a cosmic pattern. In the early 1960s, she was a student at the Fashion Institute of Technology (FIT) in New York City, where she was part of the creative circle surrounding the Greenwich Village folk music scene. Her name also appears in the archives of Western Front, a prominent artist-run center in Vancouver established in 1973, where she is listed as a performer and associated with the city's experimental and media art community in the 1970s and 1980s.
